coredata.dc:title: Achieving a better mechanical enhancing effect of carbonized polymer dots than carbon nanotubes and graphene in copper matrix
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Wenmin
Bao, Rui
Yi, Jianhong
Zhang, Yi
Pu, Youfu
Zhang, Jiatao
coredata.dc:description: Carbonized polymer dots/copper (CPD/Cu) composites were successfully prepared by a powder metallurgy process. An exhaustive comparison with reduced graphene oxide (RGO)/Cu and carbon nanotubes (CNT)/Cu composites in terms of properties, microstructure and strengthening mechanism reveal the simultaneous increase in strength and toughness of the CPD/Cu composite, which is mainly due to the CPD structure in the form of “nano-CPD ball with shell”. Meanwhile, an obtained transition area can perfectly solve the problem of dispersion and interfacial bonding of carbon reinforcements in the Cu matrix. Our results cast a new light on preparation and development of carbon nanomaterial/Cu composites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Copper composites
CPD clusters
Nano-ball structure
Mechanical properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Morphology of electrolytic Cu powder (a); the synthesized CPD (inset: particle size distribution) (b); CNT(c) and GO (d) used in this study.
Micro-structure of the CPD/Cu composite. (a–b) TEM images under different magnifications, (c) HRTEM image.
Stress-strain curves of the CPD/Cu composites (a); tensile fractography of the Cu (b); (c) CPD/Cu; (d) CNT/Cu and RGO/Cu composite (e).
UTS and EL of various copper-matrix composites with comparisons to this work.

head.ce:title: Achieving a better mechanical enhancing effect of carbonized polymer dots than carbon nanotubes and graphene in copper matrix

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming, 650093, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering
Kunming University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Faculty of Materials Science and Engineering, Kunming University of Science and Technology, Kunming 650093, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan Tin Group (Holding) Company Limited R&D Center, Kunming, Yunnan, 650106, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan Tin Group (Holding) Company Limited R&D Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yunnan Tin Group (Holding) Company Limited R&D Center, Kunming, Yunnan 650106, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbonized polymer dots/copper (CPD/Cu) composites were successfully prepared by a powder metallurgy process. An exhaustive comparison with reduced graphene oxide (RGO)/Cu and carbon nanotubes (CNT)/Cu composites in terms of properties, microstructure and strengthening mechanism reveal the simultaneous increase in strength and toughness of the CPD/Cu composite, which is mainly due to the CPD structure in the form of “nano-CPD ball with shell”. Meanwhile, an obtained transition area can perfectly solve the problem of dispersion and interfacial bonding of carbon reinforcements in the Cu matrix. Our results cast a new light on preparation and development of carbon nanomaterial/Cu composites.

ce:section.ce:para: Nano carbon materials, such as carbon nanotubes (CNT), graphene (GR) and graphene derivatives, have been intensively studied as reinforcing phases in metal matrix composites due to their unique and excellent mechanical, electrical and thermal properties very recently [1]. However, due to the poor dispersion in copper matrix as well as bad interfacial bonding between each other, the reinforcement effects of these carbon nanomaterials are still far below from the ideal expectation [2]. Nowadays, some complex-shaped parts are also needed to possess excellent mechanical properties, such as wear-resistant bearings, bending pipes, and so on [3]. In view of this, it is imperative to prepare Cu matrix composites with excellent mechanical properties.

Fortunately, carbonized polymer dots (CPD), so-called zero-dimensional derivatives of carbon, bring hope to solve the composite problem of carbon nanomaterial with metal matrix, especially with the Cu matrix. It is well known that CPD have a few to multiple layers of the GR-like microstructure in their core part, which has a spherical morphology and the width less than 10 nm [4]. More importantly, there are an abundant of surface groups such as hydroxyl, carboxyl or amino groups on their surface, which make them have good dispersion in aqueous solution and shows the characteristics of fluorescent colloid [5]. This means that we can disperse CPD into Cu salt solution, and then prepare CPD/Cu composite particles by molecular level mixing, spray pyrolysis [6] etc., to solve the problem of difficult dispersion. At the same time, a sea of functional groups on the surface can produce good interface bonding with Cu grain atoms, which have been confirmed by some studies [7]. However, this potential application value of the CPD in metal matrix has not attracted extensive attention.

Here, we prepared CPD/Cu composites by combining ball milling with spark plasma sintering (SPS). Moreover, the mechanical properties, microstructure and strengthening mechanism of the CPD/Cu composites were carefully compared with those of reduced graphene oxide (RGO)/Cu and carbon nanotube (CNT)/Cu composites prepared in the same way in detail. It was found that CPD have more advantages as reinforcement than RGO and CNT in enhancing the mechanical properties of the pure Cu matrix.

CPD was synthesized by hydrothermal method and dispersed by anhydrous ethanol according to our previously published paper [8,9]. The purchased electrolytic Cu powder (particle size 325 meshes, purity 99.9%) and pre-obtained CPD dispersion were ball milled with a variable speed (100 rpm for 480 min + 200 rpm for 300 min). Then, this mixed powder was annealed in N2+H2 atmosphere at 300 °C for 6 h and consolidated by SPS at 550 °C for 5 min in a vacuum atmosphere to obtain CPD/Cu composites. For comparative research, the RGO/Cu and CNT/Cu composites were fabricated under the same process conditions. Both of GO (0.5–5.0 μm in lateral size and 0.8–1.2 nm in thickness, purity 99.9%) and CNT (10–20 nm in diameter, 0.5–2.0 μm in length, purity 99.9%) were purchased from Chengdu Organic Chemistry Co. Ltd China. Morphology of the Cu powders and fractures surface were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Nova Namo-450, FEI). Microscopic morphology and interface microstructure of the composites were investigated by Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Tecnai G2-TF30, S-Twin). Tensile properties of the composites were measured by an AG-IS 10 KN equipment with a speed of 0.5 mm/min at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of carbon nano-materials and composites
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the microstructure morphology of the experimental raw materials including Cu powder and nano-carbon reinforcements. Fig. 1(a) gives the dendritic morphology of a typical electrolytic Cu powder. The synthesized CPD have the average diameter size ~2.20 nm and the lattice fringes of 0.21 nm correspond to the (100) crystal planes of graphite (see Fig. 1(b)). It is worth mentioning that not all of them show complete lattice fringes. The acid-treated CNT and GO used in this study were also characterized by TEM in Fig. 1(c and d). The multi-wall and hollow structure of CNT is very clear, and at the same time they show a unique bamboo structure which is also regarded as a kind of structural feature of the CNT [10]. In addition, GO exhibits a thin two-dimensional planar structure and folded structure.

Fig. 2 (a) shows the morphology of CPD/Cu composites. Due to the size difference between the CPD and Cu, the CPD is hardly to be observed in Fig. 2(a). After further magnification and observation, the CPD can be found in the Cu crystal grains and at the grain boundaries in the form of nano-clusters with a size of ~20 nm (see Fig. 2(b)), which is one magnitude larger than that of a single CPD. Besides, most of the nanoclusters exhibit an obvious “CPD ball structure with shell” (as shown in Fig. 2(b)). HRTEM image infers that the interface between the Cu and CPD clusters is clear and close-knit (in Fig. 2(c)).

The central region of these nearly spherical nanoclusters exhibits significant amorphous features, while the edge regions have obvious overlapping lattice fringes. This structure means that the nano CPD are polymerized and carbonized in the process of the composite preparation, thus forming the nearly spherical clusters. The obvious shell structure proves the good interface transition between them. These nano CPD balls are composed of amorphous carbon (AC) network embedded with numerous GR-like nanostructures. It also shows that the GR-like structure in the original CPD is still maintained in the final composite material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties of the bulk specimens
ce:section.ce:para:
Stress-strain curves of the pure Cu, RGO/Cu, CNT/Cu and CPD/Cu composites are presented in Fig. 3 (a). It is found that the CPD/Cu composite realizes excellent mechanical properties in both ultra-tensile strength (UTS) and elongation (EL), which are about 20% and 116% higher than those of pure Cu matrix, respectively. The yield point of its stress-strain curve is much higher than those of pure Cu, CNT/Cu and RGO/Cu. In addition, the results of this study are compared with those in the literature, as shown in Table 1
, only the CPD/Cu composite can improve the strength and toughness at the same time. The reinforcing effect of RGO and CNT is not as significant as CPD because the stable sp2 hybridized structure C atom is difficult to diffuse to be metallurgical bonding at a low sintering temperature of 550 °C. Very differently, nearly full densification and excellent reinforcing effect of the CPD/Cu composite can be achieved due to the special microstructure, which may be attributed to the following reasons [11]. Firstly, good dispersion in the Cu matrix: as we all know, it is difficult to avoid agglomeration and precipitation of RGO and CNT in aqueous solution even a large amount of surface dispersant is added, but CPD can dispersed very well without any surface dispersant, this will lead to a better distraction of carbon nanomaterial in the Cu matrix; secondly, good interface bonding: the RGO/Cu and CNT/Cu interfaces will produce micro-pores due to mutual insolubility, meanwhile, dislocation will generated at the interface due to the mismatch of lattice constants between the carbon material and Cu matrix. Nevertheless, for CPD/Cu, the interface bonding will be improved by the high activity AC atoms as observed in Fig. 2, which also contribute good plasticity of the composite, due to the energy dissipation of the AC network during the tensile process by sacrificing themselves [12]. Except for the uniform dispersion in the matrix and close bonding at the interface, this difference is related to the GR-like structure inside the CPD. The core part of the CPD not only hinders dislocation movement during the deformation, but also bears the load on the composites.

Tensile fractography analysis (see Fig. 3(b-e)) displays that unlike the fracture morphology of the pure Cu, all composites show many tear ridges and small tough nests, and however, the CPD/Cu composite has the most tear marks and deep dimples. Additionally, the whole dimples of the CPD/Cu composite seem to be elongated macroscopically, which may be related to the presence of AC network discussed earlier.

In summary, CPD/Cu composite were prepared successfully together with pure Cu, CNT/Cu and RGO/Cu under the same process. The nano-CPD balls are uniformly dispersed in the composites with a clear shell structure and perfectly bonded with Cu matrix. Meanwhile, the CPD are cross-linked with each other, and the existing AC network can be preferentially dissociated to release energy during the stretching process, which results in the high UTS of 312.1 MPa and EL of 34.3%. This research provides important research ideas for the development of novel carbon nano-reinforced materials and the improvement of the microstructure of C–Cu composite.

Wenmin Zhao: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Rui Bao: Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Jianhong Yi: Conceptualization, Supervision. Yi Zhang: Investigation. Youfu Pu: Validation. Jiatao Zhang: Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52174345, 52064032), the Yunnan science and technology projects (Grant No. 2019ZE001, 202002AB080001), Yunnan Ten Thousand Talents Plan Young & Elite Talents Project (YNWR-QNBJ-2018-005).
